introduction_paragraphs: Recent work on {goal} has expanded from isolated studies to a broad landscape in which core mechanisms, design assumptions, and evaluation protocols vary widely across contributions {cite_0_5}.
That breadth creates a comparison problem: similar claims are often made under different experimental setups, resource constraints, and evaluation regimes {cite_5_10}.

This survey treats {goal} as a methodological question in which approach design, underlying assumptions, and evaluation protocols have to be read together rather than as isolated contributions {cite_10_15}.

The resulting landscape is broad enough that surface-level labels alone are not informative; what matters is how a method translates its core insight into measurable outcomes and how those outcomes are validated {cite_15_20}.

We therefore organize the survey around recurring design pressures that show up across the literature, including methodology choices, efficiency trade-offs, evaluation practices, and the coupled problem of generalization and reproducibility {cite_20_25}.

Methodologically, the survey draws on {candidate_pool}, narrows that pool to {evidence_note}, and uses {time_window} as the explicit time window for reproducible retrieval and downstream evidence binding {cite_25_30}.
That evidence policy appears once here so the later sections can stay focused on technical contrasts rather than repeating caveats about evidence granularity or retrieval coverage {cite_30_35}.

The rest of the paper uses those shared lenses to compare what current approaches assume, what they optimize under comparable protocols, and where present evidence remains thin or unstable {cite_35_40}.
related_work_paragraphs: Existing survey work already covers adjacent slices of {goal}, but it often mixes taxonomies, benchmark inventories, and application snapshots without holding protocol assumptions constant {cite_0_6}.
A first strand frames the literature through foundational mechanisms, emphasizing how core techniques are formulated, combined, or extended across different settings {cite_6_12}.
A second strand centers on methodology and design, typically asking how different design decisions affect performance under controlled or constrained conditions {cite_12_18}.

A third strand focuses on representation and structure, where the main comparison is not simply whether a technique works, but which design choices remain robust as problem complexity grows {cite_18_24}.
Another line studies efficiency and scalability, comparing resource trade-offs, approximation strategies, and other mechanisms that aim to make methods practical at scale {cite_24_30}.

Evaluation-focused surveys and benchmark papers contribute a complementary view by making protocol design explicit, especially around task selection, metrics, baselines, and reproducibility {cite_30_36}.
Application-oriented work adds breadth by showing how methods transfer across domains, revealing where assumptions hold and where they break under distribution shift {cite_36_42}.

What remains under-specified across these strands is a unified lens that compares approaches, evaluation practice, and limitations under the same evidence contract rather than as separate lists {cite_42_48}.
This survey takes that gap as its starting point and positions prior work as evidence for recurring trade-offs instead of as isolated catalogs of methods, benchmarks, or applications {cite_48_54}.
abstract_sentences: Recent research on {goal} now spans multiple methodological families, design paradigms, and evaluation settings, yet those threads are still often compared under mismatched protocols {cite_0_6}.

This survey organizes the field around core mechanisms, design assumptions, efficiency trade-offs, and protocol-aware evaluation so that evidence from different approaches can be read on the same comparative scale {cite_6_12}.

Using {candidate_pool} and {evidence_note}, we synthesize how current approaches trade off effectiveness, efficiency, generality, reproducibility, and practical applicability across representative settings {cite_12_18}.
The central finding is that many reported gains depend as much on evaluation design and experimental assumptions as on the nominal methodology itself {cite_18_24}.
We conclude by identifying where current evidence is strongest, where protocol mismatch still obscures comparison, and which open questions should structure future work {cite_24_30}.
discussion_paragraphs: Across current approaches, the strongest pattern is not a single winning method but a recurring dependence on how design choices and evaluation protocols interact {cite_0_4}.
That interaction explains why apparently similar methods can report different outcomes under different assumptions, resource budgets, or evaluation setups {cite_4_8}.
It also suggests that future work should treat evaluation design, protocol transparency, and practical constraints as first-class comparative variables rather than as appendix details {cite_8_12}.
conclusion_paragraphs: Across this literature, performance is best read as evidence-bounded behavior shaped by coordinated choices about methodology, evaluation, and deployment setting {cite_12_16}.
A survey that compares approaches without making protocol assumptions explicit will overstate some gains and understate some risks {cite_16_20}.
By structuring the literature around shared lenses and comparable evidence, this comparative framing aims to make future surveys more reproducible, more stable, and easier to extend {cite_20_24}.
